title: Passion fruit sponge
subtitle: Victoria-style sponge with passion fruit curd and cream
excerpt: A classic sponge split and filled with a sharp, fragrant passion fruit curd and lightly whipped cream. The sponge is plain; the filling is the flavour. Eight passion fruit make enough curd for a generous layer.
sourceNotes: The Victoria sponge format is documented in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, as a simple pound-cake ratio enriched with baking powder. Passion fruit was introduced to British domestic baking through the influence of Caribbean and Australian cookery writing in the latter half of the twentieth century. The curd filling method follows the standard British fruit curd technique: eggs, sugar, citrus or fruit juice, butter, cooked until thick.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The passion fruit curd can be made up to 2 weeks ahead and kept in a sterilised jar in the fridge. The sponge is best baked on the day and assembled no more than 2 hours before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; two 20 cm tins bake in 22 to 25 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: Passion fruit curd sets by the coagulation of egg protein as the mixture heats. It thickens gradually between 75 and 82 degrees C. The correct consistency coats the back of a spoon and holds a line when you drag a finger across it. If it is not thick enough, cook for another 2 minutes. If it scrambles, it went over 84 degrees C; strain immediately through a fine sieve.

content.text: Passion fruit makes an assertive curd: sharp, fragrant, and slightly exotic against a plain British sponge. The
sets firmly once cold and spreads cleanly. Make it first so it has time to chill while the sponge bakes and cools.

Cut 8 passion fruits in half and scoop the pulp into a sieve over a bowl. Press to extract the juice; reserve a little of the seeds for decoration if you like. Combine the juice, 150 g caster sugar, 80 g cubed butter and 3 beaten eggs in a small saucepan over low heat. Cook, stirring constantly, until the curd thickens and coats the back of the spoon, around 10 to 12 minutes. Do not let it boil. Pour into a bowl, cover the surface with cling film and refrigerate until cold.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Grease and base-line two 20 cm round cake tins. Beat 225 g softened butter and 225 g caster sugar together until pale and fluffy. Beat in 4 eggs one at a time. Sift in 225 g self-raising flour and 1 tsp baking powder and fold in until just combined. Divide between the two tins and smooth the tops. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the sponges spring back when pressed lightly and a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tins for 5 minutes, then turn out onto a rack to cool completely.

Place one sponge layer on a plate. Spread the cold passion fruit curd generously over the top. Spread the whipped cream over the curd. Place the second sponge on top and dust with icing sugar. Serve the day it is assembled; the cream softens the sponge overnight.

items.problem: Curd is runny even after chilling
items.solution: The curd was not cooked long enough. It should coat the back of a spoon thickly before removing from the heat. Return to low heat and cook for another 3 to 5 minutes, stirring constantly.

items.problem: Sponge is dense and does not rise evenly
items.solution: The butter and sugar were not beaten long enough, or the eggs were cold. Softened butter creams easily; cold butter does not incorporate enough air. Bring eggs to room temperature before using.
